title: NOC 75200 – Taxi and limousine drivers and chauffeurs | GTR Immigration
description: NOC 75200 professionals are the essential "First-Mile/Last-Mile" connectors of Canada’s urban and regional transport systems. As a TEER 3 occupation (reclassified from Skill Level C), these drivers provide specialized passenger transportation for individuals, corporate executives, and private clients. In 2026, as Canada integrates Electric Vehicle (EV) fleets into the taxi industry and high-end tourism returns to record levels, professional chauffeurs are classified as High-Priority Service Talent. For immigration, this role is a key target for the New 2026 TR to PR Pathway and the BC PNP Entry-Level and Semi-Skilled (ELSS) stream.

classification.teer: Classification LevelDetailsBroad Occupation Category7 – Trades, transport and equipment operators and related occupationsTEER Level3Major Group75 – Helpers and labourers and other transport drivers, operators and labourersSub-Major Group752 – Other transport drivers, operators and related occupationsMinor Group7520 – Other transport drivers, operators and related occupationsNOC Code75200

duties: Passenger Transport: Pick up passengers and drive them to their destinations in a safe and timely manner.
Navigation & Logistics: Plan routes using GPS and real-time traffic updates to ensure the most efficient travel path.
Customer Service: Assist passengers with luggage, opening doors, and providing information about local points of interest or services.
Safety & Compliance: Inspect the vehicle for cleanliness and mechanical safety; ensure compliance with municipal taxi bylaws and provincial transport regulations.
Payment Processing: Record transactions, operate digital dispatch systems, and process payments (cash, credit, or corporate accounts).
Vehicle Maintenance: Perform minor vehicle maintenance, such as checking oil levels and tire pressure, and ensure the vehicle is fueled/charged and clean.
Specialized Service: (Chauffeurs) Provide personalized, high-end service to private clients, including security-conscious driving and itinerary management.
employment_requirements: Completion of secondary school is typically required.

Licensing (Mandatory): * A valid Class 4 Driver’s License (or provincial equivalent) is required to operate a taxi or limousine in most provinces.A clean driving record (Driver’s Abstract) is essential.
A clean driving record (Driver’s Abstract) is essential.

Certifications:Municipal Taxi Permit: Most cities (e.g., Toronto, Vancouver) require a specific municipal license or training certificate.Vulnerable Sector Check: Often required for paratransit or school-related transport roles.
Municipal Taxi Permit: Most cities (e.g., Toronto, Vancouver) require a specific municipal license or training certificate.
Vulnerable Sector Check: Often required for paratransit or school-related transport roles.
Good knowledge of the geography of the service area and strong interpersonal skills are mandatory.
salary_prospects: Note: Professional Chauffeurs for private families or high-level executives in 2026 often earn annual salaries between $65,000 and $85,000, which may include performance bonuses and tips.
job_outlook: The outlook is Favourable. Projections indicate that as urban centers densify and personal car ownership becomes more expensive in cities like Toronto and Vancouver, the reliance on professional transport services will increase. While autonomous vehicle technology is being tested, the high-touch, service-oriented nature of Chauffeur and Specialized Taxi roles ensures human drivers will remain the standard through 2033.
labour_market_demand: The demand for professional drivers in 2026 is Stable to Good, with a high volume of openings in major metropolitan areas. In Ontario (GTA), British Columbia (Lower Mainland), and Alberta, there is a noted shortage of licensed limousine drivers and corporate chauffeurs to support the growing luxury hospitality and business sectors. While ride-sharing apps have changed the landscape, the "Traditional Taxi" and "Private Chauffeur" sectors remain robust due to municipal regulations and the high demand for specialized, reliable executive transport.
The "Green Fleet" Transition: Drive state-of-the-art Electric Vehicles (EVs) as Canada’s taxi companies pivot to zero-emission targets by 2030.
Flexible Income Potential: Enjoy a role that often offers flexible scheduling, with high earnings during peak tourist seasons and corporate events.
Professional Networking: Chauffeurs for high-net-worth individuals and corporate executives gain unique exposure to Canada’s business and political elite.
Strategic PR Pathways: This TEER 3 role is a recognized category for several Provincial Nominee Programs (PNP) and the current TR to PR federal initiatives.
pathways: New 2026 TR to PR Pathway: A dedicated stream for 33,000 workers already in Canada, specifically targeting "Essential Transport and Service" roles.
BC PNP Entry-Level and Semi-Skilled (ELSS): A direct route for drivers currently employed in British Columbia for at least 9 months.
Alberta Opportunity Stream: Ideal for drivers with a valid job offer and provincial license working in the province.
Saskatchewan SINP (Hard-to-Fill Skills): Frequently targets drivers to support the province's regional transport needs.
